content.problemStatement:
Chef has a permutation PPP of length NNN.

Chef wants to find the number of **non-empty** subsequences of PPP which have the same number of inversions as PPP.

Since the answer can be large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

Note:


A permutation of length NNN contains all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.
The number of inversions in an array XXX is the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \le i \lt j \le N1≤i<j≤N and Xi>XjX_i \gt X_jXi​>Xj​.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting the permutation PPP.




Output Format
For each test case, output the number of non-empty subsequences of PPP which have same number of inversions as PPP, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5). The second line contains N space‑separated integers P1,P2,…,PN – a permutation of {1,…,N}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of non‑empty subsequences of the given permutation whose inversion count equals the inversion count of the whole permutation, modulo 998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: the permutation is the identity, all 5 elements are fixed points, so answer = 2^5‑1 = 31.\nTest case 2: only position 4 (value 4) is a fixed point, k=1 and not all positions are fixed, so answer = 2^1 = 2.

solution.approach:
A subsequence keeps the relative order of the chosen elements. An inversion (i,j) of the original permutation disappears if either Pi or Pj is omitted. Therefore, to retain the total number of inversions, we must keep every element that participates in at least one inversion. In a permutation an element Pi participates in an inversion unless Pi = i (a fixed point). Fixed points have no inversions with any other element because all earlier numbers are smaller and all later numbers are larger. Consequently, every valid subsequence must contain all non‑fixed points and may optionally include any subset of the fixed points. If the permutation consists only of fixed points (the identity permutation), any non‑empty subsequence works. Hence, let k be the number of fixed points. The answer is:
  • if k = N: 2^N – 1
  • otherwise: 2^k
All computations are performed modulo MOD = 998244353. The exponentiation is done in O(k) by repeated doubling (or with fast exponentiation). The algorithm scans the permutation once to count fixed points, giving O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
